texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polymer membranes were prepared by blending high density polyethylene (HDPE) with ethylene propylene diene terpolymer rubber (EPDM) and were tested for vapor permeation characteristics. The variation in sorption, diffusion, and permeation coefficients with blend composition has been studied and it was found that in these blends the permeability coefficient and sorption coefficients increased with increase in EPDM concentration. The permeation rate decreased with the increase in molecular mass of the penetrants. The permeability of the HDPE/EPDM blends vulcanized with three different vulcanizing systems has been studied and found that as the degree of crosslinking increases, the permeability decreases. The permeability measurements provide useful information on the morphology changes with composition of the blends and on the composition corresponding to the maximum level of co-continuity of the two phases.
texts.text: Polymer membranes were prepared by blending high density polyethylene (HDPE) with ethylene propylene diene terpolymer rubber (EPDM) and were tested for vapor permeation characteristics. The variation in sorption, diffusion, and permeation coefficients with blend composition has been studied and it was found that in these blends the permeability coefficient and sorption coefficients increased with increase in EPDM concentration. The permeation rate decreased with the increase in molecular mass of the penetrants. The permeability of the HDPE/EPDM blends vulcanized with three different vulcanizing systems has been studied and found that as the degree of crosslinking increases, the permeability decreases. The permeability measurements provide useful information on the morphology changes with composition of the blends and on the composition corresponding to the maximum level of co-continuity of the two phases.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: INTRODUCTION
texts.text: INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Vapor permeation has emerged as a new industrial membrane technology. In vapor permeation, the transport of a condensable vapor through a dense membrane consecutive to an activity gradient takes place. Vapor permeation offers the unique feature, from a fundamental point of view, of studying the transport process of a single permeant through a dense membrane, under various upstream concentrations. Such a characteristic can by no means be obtained by liquid permeation, where the modification of the upstream activity of a compound can only be achieved by adding another compound to the mixture. It is considered as an attractive
texts.text: Vapor permeation has emerged as a new industrial membrane technology. In vapor permeation, the transport of a condensable vapor through a dense membrane consecutive to an activity gradient takes place. Vapor permeation offers the unique feature, from a fundamental point of view, of studying the transport process of a single permeant through a dense membrane, under various upstream concentrations. Such a characteristic can by no means be obtained by liquid permeation, where the modification of the upstream activity of a compound can only be achieved by adding another compound to the mixture. It is considered as an attractive

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Received 4 June 2011; accepted 4 October 2011. Address correspondence to Sabu Thomas, Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Mahatma Gandhi University, Priyadarsini Hills, P.O, Kottayam 686560, India. Tel.: 919447223452. E-mail: sabuchathukulam@yahoo.co.uk alternative to pervaporation in which the component to be separated from a liquid feed mixture has to be vaporized through a membrane, whereas the whole feed mixture is in the vapor phase in the former. The effective membrane area requirement for pervaporation is higher than that for vapor permeation. In addition to this, the vapor permeation method offers greater simplicity in technical design and significant opportunities for energy savings (1).

The effective membrane area requirement for pervaporation is higher than that for vapor permeation. In addition to this, the vapor permeation method offers greater simplicity in technical design and significant opportunities for energy savings (1).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Besides these advantages, the study of solvent vapor permeation offers direct practical conclusion for the understanding and rational design of volatile organic components (VOC) vapor recovery from contaminated air streams. This is an energy saving process compared to classical VOC control processes such as incineration, oxidation, and active carbon absorption. In the case of VOC vapor recovery and organic extraction from aqueous stream by pervaporation, a membrane material showing high organic vapor permeabilities, but low air and water permeabilities are required in order to set up a competitive process.
texts.text: Besides these advantages, the study of solvent vapor permeation offers direct practical conclusion for the understanding and rational design of volatile organic components (VOC) vapor recovery from contaminated air streams. This is an energy saving process compared to classical VOC control processes such as incineration, oxidation, and active carbon absorption. In the case of VOC vapor recovery and organic extraction from aqueous stream by pervaporation, a membrane material showing high organic vapor permeabilities, but low air and water permeabilities are required in order to set up a competitive process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For gas and vapor separation, polymeric membranes are widely used on the industrial scale. Permeation of vapors through different polymers has been extensively studied by several researchers (2-17). Jiang et al. (18) prepared a polyurethane-poly (vinylidene fluoride) thin film composite membrane for the separation of gases. Dense polymer membranes made up from polydiemethyl siloxane (PDMS) as well as polyoctylmethylsiloxane (POMS) were used for the pervaporative separation of toluene from wastewaters by Konieczny et al. (19). Yahaya investigated the separation of mixture of benzene, toluene, ethyl benzene, and xylene, etc., from water using the newly developed composite hollow fiber membrane (20). Different ethylene-propylenediene terpolymers (EPDM) and crosslinking procedures have been evaluated in relation to pervaporation, vapor sorption, liquid sorption, and gas permeation experiments
texts.text: For gas and vapor separation, polymeric membranes are widely used on the industrial scale. Permeation of vapors through different polymers has been extensively studied by several researchers (2-17). Jiang et al. (18) prepared a polyurethane-poly (vinylidene fluoride) thin film composite membrane for the separation of gases. Dense polymer membranes made up from polydiemethyl siloxane (PDMS) as well as polyoctylmethylsiloxane (POMS) were used for the pervaporative separation of toluene from wastewaters by Konieczny et al. (19). Yahaya investigated the separation of mixture of benzene, toluene, ethyl benzene, and xylene, etc., from water using the newly developed composite hollow fiber membrane (20). Different ethylene-propylenediene terpolymers (EPDM) and crosslinking procedures have been evaluated in relation to pervaporation, vapor sorption, liquid sorption, and gas permeation experiments

texts.label: text
texts.orig: by Mouleman et al. (21). Nijhuls et al. (22) investigated many elastomers for the removal of volatile organic compounds from water. Ethylene-propylene-diene terpolymer (EPDM) showed the highest separation factor due to very low water permeability and a reasonable organic permeability.
texts.text: by Mouleman et al. (21). Nijhuls et al. (22) investigated many elastomers for the removal of volatile organic compounds from water. Ethylene-propylene-diene terpolymer (EPDM) showed the highest separation factor due to very low water permeability and a reasonable organic permeability.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Blending of polymers is a viable and versatile way to produce new materials with a wide range of properties. The phase morphology of the blend, that is the size, shape, and dispersion of the regions-whether one phase is dispersed in a continuous matrix of the other, can be an important factor in determining the properties of the blend. Vapor sorption technique is a good tool for the thermodynamic characterization of polymer blends (23). Analysis of the equilibrium sorption of a vapor by the blend can provide information regarding polymer-polymer interactions. The amount of vapor sorbed is related to its interactions with the blend. By comparing this with the interaction between the vapor and each of the pure components, it is possible to obtain information about the interaction between the polymer components in the blend.
texts.text: Blending of polymers is a viable and versatile way to produce new materials with a wide range of properties. The phase morphology of the blend, that is the size, shape, and dispersion of the regions-whether one phase is dispersed in a continuous matrix of the other, can be an important factor in determining the properties of the blend. Vapor sorption technique is a good tool for the thermodynamic characterization of polymer blends (23). Analysis of the equilibrium sorption of a vapor by the blend can provide information regarding polymer-polymer interactions. The amount of vapor sorbed is related to its interactions with the blend. By comparing this with the interaction between the vapor and each of the pure components, it is possible to obtain information about the interaction between the polymer components in the blend.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ethylene propylene diene (EPDM) elastomer has become a barrier material of significant commercial importance due to its superior resistance to thermal, oxidative, and radiation degradation coupled with its ability to accommodate high volume fractions of filler and liquid plasticizers. Despite its importance in sealing and separation applications, a fundamental understanding of transport through EPDM is still incomplete. Further investigation is required to obtain accurate, fully predictive models that are capable of estimating the permeability of vapors, liquids, and gases in this elastomer. HDPE has unique properties such as excellent mechanical properties, ozone resistance, good electrical properties, and chemical resistance but has poor stress crack resistance. Blends of HDPE and EPDM are a new class of thermoplastic elastomers, which couple the superior properties of HDPE and EPDM and open up new avenues for the commercial use of these blends (24-26). In this paper, the solvent vapor permeation properties of HDPE = EPDM blend membranes using chlorinated hydrocarbons have been analyzed. Particular attention has been given to the effects of blend ratio, nature of penetrants, and dynamic vulcanization on the permeation behavior. The experimental results were correlated to the blend morphology and compared with various theoretical predictions.
texts.text: Ethylene propylene diene (EPDM) elastomer has become a barrier material of significant commercial importance due to its superior resistance to thermal, oxidative, and radiation degradation coupled with its ability to accommodate high volume fractions of filler and liquid plasticizers. Despite its importance in sealing and separation applications, a fundamental understanding of transport through EPDM is still incomplete. Further investigation is required to obtain accurate, fully predictive models that are capable of estimating the permeability of vapors, liquids, and gases in this elastomer. HDPE has unique properties such as excellent mechanical properties, ozone resistance, good electrical properties, and chemical resistance but has poor stress crack resistance. Blends of HDPE and EPDM are a new class of thermoplastic elastomers, which couple the superior properties of HDPE and EPDM and open up new avenues for the commercial use of these blends (24-26). In this paper, the solvent vapor permeation properties of HDPE = EPDM blend membranes using chlorinated hydrocarbons have been analyzed. Particular attention has been given to the effects of blend ratio, nature of penetrants, and dynamic vulcanization on the permeation behavior. The experimental results were correlated to the blend morphology and compared with various theoretical predictions.

texts.orig: High density polyethylene (HDPE-Relene, M60 200) of density 932 kgm � 3 and melt flow index 20 g = 10 min (at 230  C = 2.16 kg) was obtained from Reliance Industries Ltd., Hazira, Gujarat, India. EPDM with an E = P ratio of 62 = 38 and a diene content of 3.92% supplied by Herdillia
texts.text: High density polyethylene (HDPE-Relene, M60 200) of density 932 kgm � 3 and melt flow index 20 g = 10 min (at 230  C = 2.16 kg) was obtained from Reliance Industries Ltd., Hazira, Gujarat, India. EPDM with an E = P ratio of 62 = 38 and a diene content of 3.92% supplied by Herdillia

texts.orig: Unimers, New Mumbai was used. The solvents Dichloromethane, Chloroform, and Carbon tetrachloride supplied by Merck India, Ltd., Mumbai, India were used. All other ingredients were of laboratory reagent grade, supplied by Bayer India, Ltd., Mumbai, India.
texts.text: Unimers, New Mumbai was used. The solvents Dichloromethane, Chloroform, and Carbon tetrachloride supplied by Merck India, Ltd., Mumbai, India were used. All other ingredients were of laboratory reagent grade, supplied by Bayer India, Ltd., Mumbai, India.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Preparation of Membranes
texts.text: Preparation of Membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The blend membranes were prepared in a Brabender plasticorder by melt mixing of the components at 160  C and a rotor speed of 60 rpm. HDPE was melted for 2 minutes and then EPDM was added. The mixing was continued for 5 minutes. Dynamically vulcanized blends were also prepared by using three different vulcanizing systems such as sulphur, peroxide(DCP), and a mixture of sulphur and peroxide (mixed). In the case of dynamically crosslinked blends, after blending HDPE with EPDM, the curing agents were added and mixing was continued for 3 minutes. Membranes were prepared by compression molding the melt mixed blends in a hydraulic press at 170  C (at 200 kg = cm 2 pressure). The thin membranes thus obtained were used for permeation experiments. The average thickness of the membrane was 0.2mm. The binary blends with varying compositions are noted as H100, H70, H50, H30, and H0 where the subscripts denote the weight % of HDPE in the blend. The formulation of the mixes used is given in Table 1.
texts.text: The blend membranes were prepared in a Brabender plasticorder by melt mixing of the components at 160  C and a rotor speed of 60 rpm. HDPE was melted for 2 minutes and then EPDM was added. The mixing was continued for 5 minutes. Dynamically vulcanized blends were also prepared by using three different vulcanizing systems such as sulphur, peroxide(DCP), and a mixture of sulphur and peroxide (mixed). In the case of dynamically crosslinked blends, after blending HDPE with EPDM, the curing agents were added and mixing was continued for 3 minutes. Membranes were prepared by compression molding the melt mixed blends in a hydraulic press at 170  C (at 200 kg = cm 2 pressure). The thin membranes thus obtained were used for permeation experiments. The average thickness of the membrane was 0.2mm. The binary blends with varying compositions are noted as H100, H70, H50, H30, and H0 where the subscripts denote the weight % of HDPE in the blend. The formulation of the mixes used is given in Table 1.

texts.orig: The crystallization behavior of the blends was determined using a Mettler 820 DSC Thermal analyzer. The first heating was done from room temperature to 200  Cat a rate of 40  C per minute followed by isothermal heating for 3 minutes and first cooling and second heating were performed at 10  C per minute in nitrogen atmosphere. The percentage crystallization was estimated from the normalized enthalpy of fusion ( D H f) using the following equation

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Vapor Permeation Studies
texts.text: Vapor Permeation Studies

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The vapor permeability was determined at room temperature by measurement of the weight loss of small vials filled with solvents and tightly closed by a membrane of 150 m m thickness. The weight loss was proportional to the time, area of the membrane, and pressure inside the vials and is inversely proportional to the thickness of the membrane.
texts.text: The vapor permeability was determined at room temperature by measurement of the weight loss of small vials filled with solvents and tightly closed by a membrane of 150 m m thickness. The weight loss was proportional to the time, area of the membrane, and pressure inside the vials and is inversely proportional to the thickness of the membrane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The permeability (P) is calculated using the following equation (27)
texts.text: The permeability (P) is calculated using the following equation (27)

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: P ¼ h h ApM ð 2 Þ

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where h is the slope of the vapor permeation graph (i.e., the quantity of the solvent sorbed vs. time), h is the thickness of the membrane, A is the area of the film used, p is the pressure difference across the membrane, and M is the molecular weight of the permeant used.
texts.text: where h is the slope of the vapor permeation graph (i.e., the quantity of the solvent sorbed vs. time), h is the thickness of the membrane, A is the area of the film used, p is the pressure difference across the membrane, and M is the molecular weight of the permeant used.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Sorption Studies
texts.text: Sorption Studies

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A circular sample of the dry membrane was vacuum dried at room temperature for 24 h. It was immersed in chlorohydrocarbons taken in diffusion bottles and kept in a thermostatically controlled oven. After attaining equilibrium, it was again taken out and the solvent adhered to the sample was removed by means of a tissue paper and quickly weighed on an electronic balance with an accuracy of  0.0001 g. Since the weighings were done within 30 s, the error associated with the evaporation of solvents is negligible. The experiments were duplicated or triplicated in most cases. The sorption coefficient (S) can be calculated from the following equation
texts.text: A circular sample of the dry membrane was vacuum dried at room temperature for 24 h. After attaining equilibrium, it was again taken out and the solvent adhered to the sample was removed by means of a tissue paper and quickly weighed on an electronic balance with an accuracy of  0.0001 g. Since the weighings were done within 30 s, the error associated with the evaporation of solvents is negligible. The experiments were duplicated or triplicated in most cases. The sorption coefficient (S) can be calculated from the following equation

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
texts.text: RESULTS AND DISCUSSION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The permeation of organic vapors through polymeric membranes is usually assumed to follow a solutiondiffusion process, that is, the penetrant molecules are first sorbed by the polymer followed by diffusion through the polymer. The net diffusion through polymer depends on the difference in the amount of penetrant molecules between the two surfaces.
texts.text: The permeation of organic vapors through polymeric membranes is usually assumed to follow a solutiondiffusion process, that is, the penetrant molecules are first sorbed by the polymer followed by diffusion through the polymer. The net diffusion through polymer depends on the difference in the amount of penetrant molecules between the two surfaces.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The factors which controls the permeability through a polymer membrane are the nature of the polymer, nature of the penetrant, solubility, pressure, temperature, concentration, and humidity. A detailed analysis of the transport process of a single penetrant can thus be achieved provided diffusion, sorption, or permeation can be studied
texts.text: The factors which controls the permeability through a polymer membrane are the nature of the polymer, nature of the penetrant, solubility, pressure, temperature, concentration, and humidity. A detailed analysis of the transport process of a single penetrant can thus be achieved provided diffusion, sorption, or permeation can be studied

texts.text: P. V. ANIL KUMAR ET AL.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: independently. But, it has been suggested that effective diffusion coefficients obtained from permeability and sorption measurements are more realistic for a correct transport description (32). At a given temperature, pressure, and concentration, the permeability of a solvent through a polymer membrane depends upon the solubility and diffusivity of the solvent molecules in the membrane. When the solubility parameter difference between the polymer and the solvent is less, the solvent is highly absorbed by the polymer. But, when the polymer has a compact network structure, the solvent diffusivity through the polymer tends to decrease.
texts.text: independently. But, it has been suggested that effective diffusion coefficients obtained from permeability and sorption measurements are more realistic for a correct transport description (32). At a given temperature, pressure, and concentration, the permeability of a solvent through a polymer membrane depends upon the solubility and diffusivity of the solvent molecules in the membrane. When the solubility parameter difference between the polymer and the solvent is less, the solvent is highly absorbed by the polymer. But, when the polymer has a compact network structure, the solvent diffusivity through the polymer tends to decrease.

texts.orig: Effect of Blend Ratio
texts.text: Effect of Blend Ratio

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figures 1 and 2 show the variation of permeation and sorption coefficients of HDPE = EPDM blends with volume fraction of EPDM. The permeation coefficients as well as sorption coefficients increased with increase of volume fraction of EPDM in the blend. The permeation coefficient of HDPE is small compared to pure EPDM. The blend compositions have intermediate values. A sharp increase in permeability is observed after 0.5 volume fraction of EPDM. The permeability and sorption coefficients for HDPE are much lower due to its inherent crystallinity. The percentage crystallinity of various systems has been given in Table 2. As the crystalline HDPE phase decreases with increase in the volume fraction of EPDM, the sorption coefficient increased and thereby the permeation coefficient. This behavior can also be related to the unique morphology of the blends. The scanning electron micrographs of H70, H50, and H30 blends are shown in Figs. 3a, 3b, and 3c, respectively. It is seen that in H70 and in H50 blends, EPDM is

The blend compositions have intermediate values. A sharp increase in permeability is observed after 0.5 volume fraction of EPDM. The permeability and sorption coefficients for HDPE are much lower due to its inherent crystallinity. The percentage crystallinity of various systems has been given in Table 2. As the crystalline HDPE phase decreases with increase in the volume fraction of EPDM, the sorption coefficient increased and thereby the permeation coefficient. This behavior can also be related to the unique morphology of the blends. The scanning electron micrographs of H70, H50, and H30 blends are shown in Figs. 3a, 3b, and 3c, respectively. It is seen that in H70 and in H50 blends, EPDM is

texts.label: text
texts.orig: dispersed as spheres in continuous HDPE matrix. The dispersed matrix morphology of H70 blend offers a more tortuous path for the penetrant, thereby reducing the diffusivity through the membrane, which in turn results in the reduction in permeability coefficient. However, as the concentration of EPDM increases from 30 to 50 wt%, the size of EPDM domains increases which increases the contact between EPDM particles and crystallinity of blends decreases and this offers a smooth passage for the penetrant through the membrane, thereby increasing the permeability coefficient. In the H30 blend, the more permeable EPDM phase becomes the continuous phase and hence the permeability coefficient increases dramatically. It was found that the permeation coefficient is higher for the dichloromethane while the sorption coefficient value is higher for the carbon tetrachloride.
texts.text: dispersed as spheres in continuous HDPE matrix. The dispersed matrix morphology of H70 blend offers a more tortuous path for the penetrant, thereby reducing the diffusivity through the membrane, which in turn results in the reduction in permeability coefficient. However, as the concentration of EPDM increases from 30 to 50 wt%, the size of EPDM domains increases which increases the contact between EPDM particles and crystallinity of blends decreases and this offers a smooth passage for the penetrant through the membrane, thereby increasing the permeability coefficient. In the H30 blend, the more permeable EPDM phase becomes the continuous phase and hence the permeability coefficient increases dramatically. It was found that the permeation coefficient is higher for the dichloromethane while the sorption coefficient value is higher for the carbon tetrachloride.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: with HDPE phase continuous, that is, in this HDPE = EPDMblends, the highly permeating EPDM forms the dispersed phase in continuous HDPE matrix. Above 0.5 volume fraction, the permeability of the blends coincides with Robeson's model, which is based on a co-continuous morphology. Hence at this volume fraction of EPDM, the blends exhibit a co-continuous morphology. Between 0.5 and 0.7 volume fraction of EPDM, there is an inflection point, which also indicates phase inversion. At 0.7 volume fraction of EPDM, the permeability of the blend is close to that for Maxwell model with the EPDM continuous phase. This indicates that HDPE = EPDM blend with 0.7 volume fraction of EPDM, the EPDM forms the continuous phase and HDPE the dispersed phase. This result is consistent with our early observation from SEM micrographs that phase inversion occurs between 50 and 70 wt% of EPDM in the blend.

Hence at this volume fraction of EPDM, the blends exhibit a co-continuous morphology. Between 0.5 and 0.7 volume fraction of EPDM, there is an inflection point, which also indicates phase inversion. At 0.7 volume fraction of EPDM, the permeability of the blend is close to that for Maxwell model with the EPDM continuous phase. This indicates that HDPE = EPDM blend with 0.7 volume fraction of EPDM, the EPDM forms the continuous phase and HDPE the dispersed phase. This result is consistent with our early observation from SEM micrographs that phase inversion occurs between 50 and 70 wt% of EPDM in the blend.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Dynamic vulcanization, the vulcanization of the rubber phase during mixing has been investigated as a method to improve the physical properties of several thermoplastic elastomers based on rubber = plastic blends. During dynamic vulcanization the crosslinked rubber phase becomes finely and uniformly distributed in the plastic

texts.label: text
texts.orig: matrix and attains a stable morphology as shown in Fig. 5. In the present study three vulcanizing systems are used. These include sulphur, peroxide (DCP), and a mixture of sulphur and peroxide (MIXED). The variation of permeability of the H50 blend crosslinked with these three vulcanizing systems are shown in Fig. 6. Permeability decreases from H50 to peroxide vulcanized sample. This difference in behavior can be explained on the basis of crosslink density of the vulcanized samples. Calculated values of the crosslink densities are given in Table 3. The degree of crosslinking is minimum for the H50 blend (physical entanglements) and the peroxide (DCP) system the highest. As the degree of crosslinking increases the sorption coefficient and diffusion coefficients decrease and hence the permeation coefficients.

This difference in behavior can be explained on the basis of crosslink density of the vulcanized samples. Calculated values of the crosslink densities are given in Table 3. The degree of crosslinking is minimum for the H50 blend (physical entanglements) and the peroxide (DCP) system the highest. As the degree of crosslinking increases the sorption coefficient and diffusion coefficients decrease and hence the permeation coefficients.

texts.orig: CHLORINATED HYDROCARBON VAPORS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The penetrants used were dichloromethane (CH2Cl2), chloroform (CHCl3), and carbon tetrachloride (CCl4). Dichloromethane vapors were found to permeate fast through the membrane. This is due to low molecular size of dichloromethane molecules, which can easily pass through the free volumes of the membrane as compared to the large molecules of chloroform and carbon tetrachloride. Thus the order of amount of vapor permeated has been found according to the penetrant size, that is, dichloromethane > chloroform > carbon tetrachloride.
texts.text: The penetrants used were dichloromethane (CH2Cl2), chloroform (CHCl3), and carbon tetrachloride (CCl4). Dichloromethane vapors were found to permeate fast through the membrane. This is due to low molecular size of dichloromethane molecules, which can easily pass through the free volumes of the membrane as compared to the large molecules of chloroform and carbon tetrachloride. Thus the order of amount of vapor permeated has been found according to the penetrant size, that is, dichloromethane > chloroform > carbon tetrachloride.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: CONCLUSION
texts.text: CONCLUSION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Permeation of three aliphatic chlorinated solvent vapors viz., dichloromethane, chloroform, and carbon tetrachloride through high density polyethylene, ethylene propylene diene terpolymer rubber and their blends has been studied with reference to the effect of blend ratio. Solvent vapor permeation increased with the volume fraction of EPDM. This is because of the reduction in crystallinity with increase in EPDM content and also due to the morphological transformations. The morphology and the values of % crystallinity are complementary to the observations. In the case of dynamically vulcanized blends, as the degree of crosslinking increases the sorption coefficient and diffusion coefficients decrease and hence the permeation coefficients. Of the three aliphatic vapors used, the observed results are in the order dichloromethane > chloroform > carbon tetrachloride. This is in accordance with their molecular size.
texts.text: Permeation of three aliphatic chlorinated solvent vapors viz., dichloromethane, chloroform, and carbon tetrachloride through high density polyethylene, ethylene propylene diene terpolymer rubber and their blends has been studied with reference to the effect of blend ratio. Solvent vapor permeation increased with the volume fraction of EPDM. This is because of the reduction in crystallinity with increase in EPDM content and also due to the morphological transformations. The morphology and the values of % crystallinity are complementary to the observations. In the case of dynamically vulcanized blends, as the degree of crosslinking increases the sorption coefficient and diffusion coefficients decrease and hence the permeation coefficients. Of the three aliphatic vapors used, the observed results are in the order dichloromethane > chloroform > carbon tetrachloride. This is in accordance with their molecular size.
